It is sad to think how much sin is committed in great cities. Their wickedness, like that of Nineveh, is an open and bold offense to God. Jonah must go immediately to Nineveh, and there, on the spot, cry against its wickedness. Jonah would not go. Probably few among us would not have tried to avoid such a mission. Providence seemed to give him an opportunity to escape; we may be out of the way of duty, yet still experience favorable conditions. The easiest path is not always the right path. See what even the best people are like when God leaves them to themselves; and how much we need, when the word of the Lord comes to us, for the Spirit of the Lord to bring every thought within us into obedience.

God sent a pursuer after Jonah, namely, a mighty tempest. Sin brings storms and tempests into the soul, into families, churches, and nations; it is disturbing and unsettling. Having called upon their gods for help, the sailors did what they could to assist themselves. Oh that men would be wise concerning their souls, and would be willing to give up wealth, pleasure, and honor, which they cannot keep without losing faith and a good conscience, and ruining their souls forever! Jonah was fast asleep. Sin is stupefying, and we must take care lest at any time our hearts are hardened by its deceitfulness. What do people mean by continuing in sin when the word of God and the convictions of their own consciences warn them to arise and call on the Lord if they want to escape everlasting misery? Should we not warn each other to awake, to rise up, to call upon our God, so that he may deliver us? The sailors concluded that the storm was a messenger of Divine justice sent to someone on that ship. Whatever evil comes upon us at any time, there is a cause for it; and each must pray, Lord, show me why you contend with me. The lot fell upon Jonah. God has many ways of bringing hidden sins and sinners to light, and making manifest what was thought to be hidden from the eyes of all living.

Jonah gave an account of his religion, for that was his duty. We may hope that he told it with sorrow and shame, justifying God, condemning himself, and explaining to the mariners how great God Jehovah is. They said to him, Why did you do this? If you fear the God who made the sea and dry land, why were you such a fool as to think you could flee from his presence? If people who profess religion do wrong, they will hear it from those who do not make such professions. When sin has raised a storm, and laid us under tokens of God’s displeasure, we must consider what is to be done about the sin that raised the storm. Jonah uses the language of true penitents, who desire that no one else should suffer for their sins and follies. Jonah sees this as the punishment for his iniquity; he accepts it, and justifies God in it. When conscience is awakened, and a storm is raised, nothing will turn it into calm except parting with the sin that caused the disturbance. Giving up our money will not pacify the conscience; Jonah must be thrown overboard.

The mariners rowed against wind and tide, the wind of God's displeasure, the tide of his counsel; but it is in vain to think of saving ourselves any other way than by destroying our sins. Even natural conscience cannot but dread blood-guiltiness. And when we are led by Providence, God does what he pleases, and we ought to be satisfied, even if it doesn't please us. Throwing Jonah into the sea put an end to the storm. God will not afflict forever; He will only contend until we submit and turn from our sins. Surely these heathen mariners will rise up in judgment against many called Christians, who neither offer prayers when in distress nor give thanks for signal deliverances. The Lord commands all creatures, and can make any of them serve his designs of mercy to his people. Let us see this salvation of the Lord, and admire his power, that he could thus save a drowning man, and his pity, that he would thus save one who was running from him and had offended him. It was by the Lord’s mercies that Jonah was not consumed. Jonah was alive in the fish for three days and nights: to nature this was impossible, but to the God of nature all things are possible. Jonah, by this miraculous preservation, was made a type of Christ; as our blessed Lord himself declared.
